paragraphs.text: Dear Sir or Madam, I am writing as a concerned consumer and dedicated video game enthusiast to respectfully raise several issues regarding current industry practices that I believe are anti-consumer and should be addressed under any current or future Digital Fairness legislation. 1. Physical Games Should Contain Full Game Data More and more publishers are now selling physical copies of games on discs or cartridges that do not contain the full game data. Instead, they act only as activation keys, requiring massive online downloads before a game can be played. This misleads customers who expect a complete, playable product. I believe physical media should contain the entire game, as traditionally expected. 2. Stop Mandatory Online Access for Single-Player Content Many single-player games now require a constant internet connection even though no multiplayer features are used. This is unreasonable and harms players who may not have consistent internet access or who simply prefer to play offline. Players should be allowed to access all single-player features without needing to be connected online. 3. Game Consoles Should Work Offline Similarly, game consoles today often require an internet connection just to function or to play purchased games. This limits how, when, and where players can use their own hardware and software. Basic console functionality and access to previously downloaded or installed games should always be available offline. 4. Publishers Must Stop Shutting Down Purchased Games It is extremely unfair when publishers disable or shut down games that people have paid for, especially online-only titles with no offline mode. When someone buys a game, they should be guaranteed lasting access to that product. I strongly urge regulations to prevent companies from removing access to paid games, particularly those with no alternative modes of play. 5. Excessive Data Collection Must Be Regulated Some publishers collect large amounts of personal and gameplay data without clearly informing users or providing meaningful consent options. In some cases, this even affects game performance. While diagnostics may be useful, such data collection must be transparent, limited, and respectful of user privacy. These practices, including limited access to paid products, forced online connections, incomplete physical media, and excessive data collection, are deeply anti-consumer. They erode trust, reduce digital ownership, and put unnecessary restrictions on honest customers. I respectfully urge you to consider these concerns when shaping the future of digital fairness laws. Gamers deserve better protections, honest products, and the right to use what they purchase without unreasonable limitations. Thank you very much for your time and attention to these important matters. Sincerely, Ash A Concerned Gamer and Consumer
